title: Convolutions Deep Dive Capstone: What We've Built
concept: Across eight units — from the basic convolution operation through stride, padding, feature maps, 1x1 convs, dilated and depthwise-separable convs, grouped convs, transposed convs, and the output-size formula that ties it all together — you've built the complete toolkit for how convolutional layers see, resize, and reshape images.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why does output-size math matter for real architecture design, not just as an abstract exercise?

options.text: Because mismatched or shrinking-to-zero feature maps can break a network before it ever trains — the formula lets you catch that in advance
It doesn't — output size is purely cosmetic and has no real effect
Only because it affects file size on disk

exercises.explain: Exactly. Now let's zoom all the way out and see the entire journey you've just completed.

cards.title: Look how far you've come
cards.body: Eight units ago, a convolution was a mystery operation sliding a grid of numbers over an image. Now you can explain exactly what it does, control its every dial, and calculate precisely what shape comes out the other end. Let's walk the whole path you just built, start to finish.

cards.title: The full arc, unit by unit
cards.body: Unit 97 introduced the convolution operation itself — a learned filter sliding over pixels. Unit 98 added stride and padding, controlling how far the filter steps and how its edges are handled. Unit 99 showed what comes out the other side: feature maps, stacked in layers. Unit 100 introduced the 1x1 convolution, mixing channels without touching space. Unit 101 stretched a filter's reach with dilation, and split it apart with depthwise-separable convs. Unit 102 split filters into independent groups. Unit 103 flipped the direction entirely with transposed convolutions, upsampling instead of shrinking. Unit 104 — this unit — gave you the exact formula tying every one of those choices to a precise output size.

cards.title: A fully stocked toolbox
cards.body: You started this phase with one tool: a plain convolution. You're leaving it with a full toolbox — a wrench for stride, a shim for padding, a magnifier for dilation, a splitter for grouped and depthwise-separable convs, a reverse gear for transposed convs, and a tape measure — the output-size formula — that tells you exactly what any of them will build before you ever run the code.

cards.title: One layer, every idea at once
cards.body: Picture a single real layer: a grouped, depthwise-separable convolution with dilation 2 and stride 2, followed by same-padding to hold the size steady. Every concept from this phase shows up in that one line of a real architecture — and the output-size formula is what tells you, before training even starts, exactly what shape comes out.

cards.title: This wasn't eight separate topics
cards.body: It's easy to look back and think you learned eight unrelated tricks. You didn't — every idea in this phase answers the same underlying question: how does a filter turn one grid of numbers into another? Stride, padding, dilation, grouping, and transposing are just different answers to 'where does the filter look, and how far does it move,' and the output-size formula is simply the arithmetic that describes all of them at once.

cards.title: Where You Started vs. Where You Are Now
cards.body: The distance covered in one phase:

left.text:
One filter,
one sliding operation,
output size unknown

right.text:
Stride, padding, dilation,
grouping, transposing —
output size exact, by formula

exercises.prompt: Which unit in this phase introduced upsampling — going from a smaller feature map to a larger one?
exercises.explain: Right — Unit 103's transposed convolutions were built specifically to upsample, going from a smaller feature map to a larger one.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Grouped convolutions and depthwise-separable convolutions were both about splitting up channels rather than changing spatial size.
exercises.explain: True — Unit 101's depthwise-separable convs and Unit 102's grouped convs both split channel processing apart; neither is primarily about resizing the feature map spatially.

exercises.sentence: Even for a dilated filter, the output-size formula still applies — you just swap in the effective size, which accounts for the filter's full ___, gaps included.
exercises.explain: Footprint — dilation doesn't change the weight count, only the space the filter's footprint actually covers, which is exactly what the effective-size formula captures.

exercises.prompt: Which statement best captures the throughline connecting Units 97 through 104?

options.text: Every unit answered the same question — where does a filter look and how far does it move — through a different dial: stride, padding, dilation, grouping, or direction
Each unit was an unrelated, standalone topic with no shared theme running through them
The units were only ever about reducing image file size for storage

exercises.explain: Exactly — one underlying question, answered through several different dials, with the output-size formula as the math tying every answer together.

exercises.prompt: Reflecting on Units 97 through 104: a plain convolution, stride and padding, feature maps, 1x1 convs, dilated and depthwise-separable convs, grouped convs, transposed convs, and the output-size formula — what's the single thread tying all of it together?

options.text: Every one of them is a different way of controlling how a filter reads and reshapes an image — and the output-size formula is the exact math that predicts the result of any combination of those choices
They're eight completely separate algorithms with no shared logic between any of them
Only the output-size formula matters; everything before it — stride, padding, dilation, grouping — was just background trivia
The phase was really about image file formats, not about convolutions at all

exercises.explain: Exactly right. Every unit in this phase — the plain convolution, stride and padding, feature maps, 1x1 convs, dilation and depthwise-separable convs, grouped convs, and transposed convs — is a different lever on the same machine, and the output-size formula is the math that predicts what any combination of those levers produces. Phase 2: Convolutions Deep Dive is complete. Next up: Pooling & CNN Building Blocks.
